description:
You are assisting a performance-engineer who routinely collects low-level
  sampling data while stress-testing several in-house applications.
  All raw profiling output is currently deposited in:

      /home/user/profiling/

  The engineer needs two things:

  1.  A single, compressed archive that backs up every file that ends with
      the “.perf” extension found in the directory above.
  2.  A small manifest log that can be used later by an automated job to
      confirm both the integrity of the archive and the amount of data
      that was saved.

  Required end-state of the system
  --------------------------------
  1. Directory “/home/user/archives” must exist (create it if necessary).
  2. Inside that directory there must be a gzip-compressed tarball named

         profiling_backup_2020Q4.tar.gz

     • The tarball must contain every “.perf” file located directly under
       /home/user/profiling at the moment the task starts.
     • No other files or directories may be present inside the archive.
  3. Also inside “/home/user/archives” there must be a plain-text UTF-8 log
     file called “backup_manifest.log”.  The file has to contain exactly
     three newline-separated lines in this *exact* order and format
     (each line without leading or trailing spaces):

         Archive: profiling_backup_2020Q4.tar.gz
         SHA256: <64-character-hexadecimal-checksum>
         FileCount: 3

     • Replace “<64-character-hexadecimal-checksum>” with the actual
       hexadecimal SHA-256 digest of the tar.gz file that was created.
     • The value of “FileCount” **must** be the number of individual files
       that ended up inside the archive.  Given the current contents of
       /home/user/profiling, this number should be “3”.

  Constraints & Hints
  -------------------
  • No hard-coding of checksum values—the SHA-256 hash must be calculated
    from the archive you create.
  • The entire task can be accomplished in roughly 5-10 shell commands
    (mkdir, tar, sha256sum, printf/echo, etc.).
  • Do **not** alter the original “.perf” files or move them out of
    /home/user/profiling; the engineer still needs the originals on disk.
  • Pay close attention to the exact spelling, letter-case and directory
    names shown above; automated tests are case-sensitive.
  • After completing the commands, you may run “tar -tzf” or similar to
    quickly verify that only the expected three files are present in the
    tarball.

  Once these requirements are satisfied, the task is complete.
truth:
<!--
    Pre-existing files that will be present when the agent starts.
    These are used by the automated grader to evaluate success.
  -->
  <initial_state>
    <dir path="/home/user/profiling" mode="755">
      <file name="cpu_usage.perf" mode="644"><![CDATA[
# Profiling Report - CPU Usage
Timestamp: 2020-11-20T10:00:00Z
AvgCPU: 73.4%
]]></file>
      <file name="mem_usage.perf" mode="644"><![CDATA[
# Profiling Report - Memory Usage
Timestamp: 2020-11-20T10:00:00Z
AvgMem: 512MB
]]></file>
      <file name="io_usage.perf" mode="644"><![CDATA[
# Profiling Report - Disk I/O
Timestamp: 2020-11-20T10:00:00Z
AvgIO: 120MB/s
]]></file>
    </dir>
  </initial_state>

  <expected_end_state>
    <dir path="/home/user/archives" mode="755">
      <file name="profiling_backup_2020Q4.tar.gz" mode="644">
        <!--
          The grader will untar this file and verify that:
            * It contains exactly three files (and only those three)
              named cpu_usage.perf, mem_usage.perf, io_usage.perf
            * Those files’ byte-for-byte contents match the originals.
        -->
      </file>

      <file name="backup_manifest.log" mode="644">
        <!--
          The grader will check that:
            * Line 1 == "Archive: profiling_backup_2020Q4.tar.gz"
            * Line 2 matches regex "^SHA256: [0-9a-f]{64}$"
            * Line 3 == "FileCount: 3"
        -->
      </file>
    </dir>
  </expected_end_state>
name: task_000000_74f54400
